title: Charmed Life — 2 (Fragment 3)
sourceText:
“But what of your dear mother?” persisted Mr. Nostrum, his odd eye traveling away, while his brother managed to stare gloomily at the pavement and the rooftops at once.

“You can see the poor boy knows nothing, Henry,” Mr. William said. “I doubt if he would be able to tell us his dear mother’s maiden name.”

“Oh, I do know that,” said Cat. “It’s on their marriage lines. She was called Chant too.”

“Odd,” said Mr. Nostrum, swirling an eye at his brother.

“Odd, and peculiarly unhelpful,” Mr. William agreed.

Cat wanted to get away. He felt he had taken enough strange questions to last till Christmas. “Well, if you want to know that badly,” he said, “why don’t you write and ask Mr.—er—Mr. Chres—”

“Hush!” said Mr. Henry Nostrum violently.

“Hum!” said his brother, almost equally violently.

“August Personage, I mean,” Cat said, looking at Mr. William in alarm. Mr. William’s eyes had gone right to the sides of his face. Cat was afraid he might be going off into a trance, like Miss Larkins.

“It will serve, Henry, it will serve!” Mr. William cried out. And, with great triumph, he lifted the silver watch-chain off his middle and shook it. “Then for silver!” he cried.

“I’m so glad,” Cat said politely. “I have to be going now.” He ran off down the street as fast as he could. When he went out that afternoon, he took care to turn right and go out of Coven Street past the Willing Warlock’s house. It was rather a nuisance, since that was the long way around to where most of his friends lived, but anything was better than meeting Miss Larkins or the Nostrums again. It was almost enough to make Cat wish that school had started.

When Cat came home that evening, Gwendolen was just back from her lesson with Mr. Nostrum. She had her usual glowing, exulting look, but she was looking secretive and important too.

“That was a good idea of yours of writing to Chrestomanci,” she said to Cat. “I can’t think why I didn’t think of it. Anyway, I just have.”

“Why did you do it? Couldn’t Mr. Nostrum?” Cat asked.

“It came more naturally from me,” said Gwendolen. “And I suppose it doesn’t matter if he gets my signature. Mr. Nostrum told me what to write.”

“Why does he want to know anyway?” Cat said.

“Wouldn’t you like to know!” Gwendolen said exultingly.

“No,” said Cat. “I wouldn’t.” Since this had brought what happened that morning into his mind, which still made him almost wish the Autumn term had started, he said, “I wish the horse chestnuts were ripe.”

“Horse chestnuts!” Gwendolen said, in the greatest disgust. “What a low mind you have! They won’t be ready for a good six weeks.”

“I know,” said Cat, and for the next two days he carefully turned right every time he left the house.

They were the lovely golden days that happen when August is passing into September. Cat and his friends went out along the river. On the second day, they found a wall and climbed it. There was an orchard beyond, and here they were lucky enough to discover a tree loaded with sweet white apples—the kind that ripen early. They filled their pockets and then their hats. Then a furious gardener chased them with a rake. They ran. Cat was very happy as he carried his full, knobby hat home. Mrs. Sharp loved apples. He just hoped she would not reward him by making gingerbread men. As a rule, gingerbread men were fun. They leaped up off the plate and ran when you tried to eat them, so that when you finally caught them you felt quite justified in eating them. It was a fair fight, and some got away. But Mrs. Sharp’s gingerbread men never did that. They simply lay, feebly waving their arms, and Cat never had the heart to eat them.

words.definition: to continue firmly or obstinately in an opinion or a course of action in spite of difficulty or opposition
words.example: “But what of your dear mother?” persisted Mr. Nostrum.
words.exampleText: persisted means continued firmly despite difficulty.

words.definition: in a way that is dark or poorly lit, often suggesting sadness or melancholy
words.example: his brother managed to stare gloomily at the pavement
words.exampleText: gloomily describes looking in a sad or dark manner.

words.definition: the surname a woman has from birth before she may have taken a married name
words.example: I doubt if he would be able to tell us his dear mother’s maiden name.
words.exampleText: refers to a woman's original surname.

words.definition: moving in a twisting or spiraling pattern
words.example: Odd, said Mr. Nostrum, swirling an eye at his brother.
words.exampleText: swirling means moving in a twisting or circular motion.

words.definition: in a strange or unusual way
words.example: Odd, and peculiarly unhelpful, Mr. William agreed.
words.exampleText: used to indicate something unusual or strange.

words.definition: not providing assistance or support; disadvantageous
words.example: Odd, and peculiarly unhelpful, Mr. William agreed.
words.exampleText: describes something not providing help.

words.definition: a half-conscious state often induced by hypnosis or emotion
words.example: he might be going off into a trance, like Miss Larkins.
words.exampleText: a dazed or altered state of consciousness.

words.definition: a great victory or achievement
words.example: With great triumph, he lifted the silver watch-chain.
words.exampleText: a feeling or act of winning or success.

words.definition: a decorative chain used to attach a pocket watch
words.example: He lifted the silver watch-chain off his middle and shook it.
words.exampleText: a chain for holding a pocket watch.

words.definition: a person or thing causing inconvenience or annoyance
words.example: It was rather a nuisance since that was the long way around.
words.exampleText: something annoying or troublesome.

words.definition: inclined to conceal feelings and intentions or not to disclose information
words.example: She was looking secretive and important.
words.exampleText: tending not to reveal information.

words.definition: showing or feeling elation or jubilation, especially as the result of a success
words.example: She had her usual glowing, exulting look.
words.exampleText: expressing great joy or triumph.

words.definition: a person's name written in a distinctive way as a form of identification
words.example: I just have (written to Chrestomanci); it doesn’t matter if he gets my signature.
words.exampleText: a person's handwritten name.

words.definition: in a way that shows great joy or triumph
words.example: Gwendolen said exultingly.
words.exampleText: expressing triumphant joy.

words.definition: a strong feeling of dislike or disapproval
words.example: Horse chestnuts! Gwendolen said in the greatest disgust.
words.exampleText: a strong negative reaction.

words.definition: having lumps or knobs
words.example: Cat was very happy as he carried his full, knobby hat home.
words.exampleText: bumpy or having small protrusions.

words.definition: very angry
words.example: A furious gardener chased them with a rake.
words.exampleText: extremely angry or intense.

words.definition: a person who tends and cultivates a garden as a pastime or profession
words.example: A furious gardener chased them.
words.exampleText: someone who cares for plants and gardens.

words.definition: to give something in recognition of service, effort, or achievement
words.example: He hoped she would not reward him by making gingerbread men.
words.exampleText: to give something in return for good behavior or effort.

words.definition: cookie figures made from gingerbread dough shaped like little men
words.example: Mrs. Sharp’s gingerbread men never did that.
words.exampleText: traditional spiced cookies shaped like people.

words.definition: in a way that lacks strength or force
words.example: They simply lay, feebly waving their arms.
words.exampleText: done weakly or without strength.

words.definition: having a good reason for an action or belief
words.example: You felt quite justified in eating them.
words.exampleText: having a valid or acceptable reason.

words.definition: jumped or sprang a long way, to a great height, or with great force
words.example: They leaped up off the plate and ran when you tried to eat them.
words.exampleText: jumped suddenly with force.

words.definition: words that often go together and sound natural to native speakers
words.example: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations are important.
words.exampleText: common word pairings or phrases.
